title: Cylindrical Matter: A beyond-quantum many-body system for efficient classical simulation of quantum pure-Ising like systems
authors: Sahar Atallah, Peter Carrekmor, Michael Garn, Yukuan Tao, Shashank Virmani
abstract: Even simplified models of quantum many-body systems can be difficult to analyse. However, taking inspiration from the foundations of physics, one may wonder whether there are practical advantages to constructing alternative beyond-quantum descriptions of many-body systems. We explore this question in the context of quantum interactions that are diagonal in the computational basis. We construct a hypothetical model of a continuous time dynamical many-body system that is based upon lattices of interacting particles called "cylindrical bits", a concept first introduced in [6]. In the language of [5] our toy model is {\it non-free}, as we need spatial constraints on how the particles interact to ensure valid probabilities. We investigate these constraints and explore the resulting `entangled' states that can exist. Certain pure {\it quantum} entangled systems can be faithfully mimicked by our cylindrical worlds. This allows us to simulate efficiently classically, in the sense of sampling measurement outcomes, a variety of previously unknown quantum systems. Examples include some states created by pure Ising interactions algebraically decaying faster than $\sim 1/r^{3D/2}$, with spatial dimension $D$, under measurements in the $Z$ eigenbasis or eigenbases of $aX+bY$ for $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$. We also explore whether another choice of non-quantum `particle' could expand the applicability of the classical simulation by defining and partially optimising a figure-of-merit that attempts to capture how useful various possibilities may be.
summary: This paper introduces 'cylindrical matter,' a beyond-quantum many-body model that allows for the efficient classical simulation of specific quantum Ising-like systems. By using a state space that extends beyond the Bloch sphere, the authors find a way to sample measurement outcomes of previously hard-to-simulate quantum states. The work establishes precise conditions on interaction decay and dimensionality for this classical simulation to remain efficient.
main_problem: Determining the boundaries of classical simulability for quantum many-body systems by exploring whether 'beyond-quantum' descriptions can provide efficient simulation advantages.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a hypothetical 'cylindrical matter' model can efficiently classically simulate certain previously unknown quantum pure-Ising-like systems with power-law decaying interactions.
method: The study uses a framework of 'cylindrical bits' and 'generalized separability' to analyze the growth of state space radii under diagonal unitaries, employing analytical proofs and numerical linear programming.
model_or_system: A continuous-time many-body system of 'cylindrical bits' on a lattice, where each particle's state space is a cylinder in the Bloch vector space, subject to diagonal two-body interactions and adaptive measurements.
key_observables: Measurement outcomes in the Z eigenbasis and the XY plane (aX + bY).
important_parameters: Cylindrical radius (r), interaction decay exponent (alpha), spatial dimension (D), and the growth factor (lambda) required to maintain separability.
main_assumptions: The model is 'non-free' due to required spatial constraints; measurements are primarily destructive or quasi-destructive; and the simulation assumes a finite gate set to ensure bounded fan-in.
figures_summary: Figure 1 sketches the cylindrical state space protruding from the Bloch sphere; Figure 2 plots the growth factor as a function of gate phase; Figure 3 shows the tradeoff between the initial state angle and the power-law decay rate.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the cylindrical bit model, defines the mathematical framework for generalized separability, analyzes the radial growth required for diagonal gates, establishes simulation thresholds for power-law interactions, and compares the optimality of cylindrical state spaces against alternatives.
why_interesting: It provides a novel way to understand the limits of classical simulation by using non-quantum models to mimic complex quantum entanglement, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and the study of computational complexity in quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A sketch of Cyl(1) in the space of Bloch vectors. For any value of r the cylinder Cyl(r) protrudes from the Bloch sphere and hence always contain pure states.

FIG. 2. The plot shows λ(φ). We do not plot φ ∈(π, 2π) as φ and 2π −φ give the same values - the graph would be symmetric about the axis φ = π. At φ = 0 no growth is needed, as the gate is trivially the identity gate, and for φ = π we recover the value derived for the CZ gate in [6]).

FIG. 3. The horizontal axis is α, the power law decay rate for a one dimensional system. The vertical axis is the largest an- gle θ in degrees, such that initial states cos(θ/2)|0⟩+sin θ/2|1⟩ are within the initial radius accepted by the classical simula- tion algorithm. In other words, if states with these values of θ or lower are interacted with the long range gates, the algorithm can eﬃciently sample the outcomes of cylindrical measurements in the limit of large numbers of particles. For this graph the gate between a qubit and its immediate neigh- bour is the CZ gate. For any ﬁnite system in practice one will be able to simulate more inputs, possibly also by exploiting asymmetric growth...

FIG. 4. The θ (vertical axis), φ (horizontal axis) phase space. A particular point (φ, θ) hence represents the pure states one gets by placing states (I+sin(θ)X+cos(θ)Z)/2 on the nodes of a graph and interacting them with arbitrary two-qubit diago- nal gates eiφ1|00⟩⟨00|+eiφ2|01⟩⟨01|+eiφ3|10⟩⟨10|+eiφ4|11⟩⟨11| such that φ = φ4 +φ1−φ2−φ3. The upper curve corresponds to graphs with degree ∆= 3 and the lower curve corresponds to graphs with degree ∆= 4. In each case for a given value of φ the system is classically eﬃciently simulatable for pure states with polar angles θ that are below the curve (mixed states that are convex mixtures of those pure states are also classically eﬃcient, but can...

FIG. 5. An illustration of the coarse graining procedure. Imagine that this is a 4 × 4 block of cylinders within a larger square lattice. We create a separable decomposition between it and the rest of the lattice by growing the radii of the bound- ary cylinders in accordance with how many interactions they have with the rest of the system. The corner cylinders hence have larger radius than the other cylinders on the boundary.
